summary: Deuteronomy exhibits extensive theological revision across traditions, particularly anthropomorphism avoidance in Targums (consistently replacing divine body language with Glory/Memra theology), harmonization in LXX and SP (aligning legal and narrative material with Pentateuchal parallels), and proto-rabbinic adjustments in Targums reflecting early halakhic interpretation. The LXX demonstrates theological softening of violent conquest imagery and occasional eschatological sharpening in prophetic-liturgical passages.
summary_plain: Ancient translators and scribes modified Deuteronomy to avoid describing God in human terms, to smooth out contradictions with other biblical books, and to align the text with emerging Jewish legal interpretations. Greek and Aramaic versions especially show efforts to make troubling conquest narratives less offensive and to interpret ambiguous passages as referring to future messianic hope.

revisions.mt_reading: which YHWH your God has allotted (ḥālaq) to all the peoples
revisions.revised_reading: which the Lord your God showed (ἔδειξεν) to all the nations
revisions.evidence: MT's ḥālaq implies God assigned/distributed celestial bodies to nations as legitimate objects of worship, creating theological tension. LXX's ἔδειξεν (showed/displayed) removes the implication of divine authorization for astral cult, instead suggesting God merely made them visible. This represents theological correction to avoid suggesting YHWH endorsed idolatry among nations.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew implies God gave the stars to other nations to worship, which seemed to justify idolatry. The Greek changes this to say God merely showed the stars, removing any hint of divine approval for star-worship.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Deuteronomy, p. 77
Dogniez & Harl, La Bible d'Alexandrie: Le Deutéronome, p. 130
revisions.counter_arguments: ἔδειξεν could be a straightforward lexical choice for ḥālaq without theological motivation, simply emphasizing divine creative display rather than distribution.

revisions.mt_reading: Know therefore today that YHWH your God is the one who crosses over (ʿōbēr) before you
revisions.revised_reading: the Memra of the LORD your God, it is that which passes over at your head
revisions.evidence: Targum Onqelos systematically inserts Memra (divine Word) to avoid direct divine physical movement. MT depicts YHWH personally crossing Jordan as military leader; Targum relocates agency to hypostasized Memra. This reflects proto-rabbinic theology distancing transcendent God from spatial locomotion while preserving efficacy of divine presence.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew describes God himself physically crossing the Jordan River ahead of Israel. The Aramaic translation inserts 'the Word of the Lord' as an intermediary to avoid saying God literally moved through space.
scholarly_citations: Hayward, The Targum of Deuteronomy, p. 35
Maher, Targum Pseudo-Jonathan: Deuteronomy, p. 38
revisions.counter_arguments: Memra may function primarily as stylistic reverence marker rather than strictly anti-anthropomorphic theology, as Targums are inconsistent in its application.

revisions.mt_reading: according to the number of the sons of God (bənê ʾĕlōhîm)
revisions.revised_reading: according to the number of the angels of God (ἀγγέλων θεοῦ)
revisions.evidence: MT (confirmed by 4QDeut) reads 'sons of God/El,' implying divine council theology where YHWH receives Israel among pantheon divisions. LXX's 'angels' reinterprets potentially polytheistic bənê ʾĕlōhîm through angelological framework compatible with monotheism. MT tradition also has 'sons of Israel' (harmonization to avoid divine plurality); LXX mediates with angelic intermediaries.
revisions.evidence_plain: The original Hebrew referred to 'sons of God' (divine beings) dividing nations, which could sound polytheistic. The Greek translation changed this to 'angels of God' to fit strict monotheism and avoid implying multiple gods.
scholarly_citations: Skehan, A Fragment of the Song of Moses, JBL 1954
van der Kooij, The Ending of the Song of Moses, p. 271
revisions.counter_arguments: Some LXX manuscripts read 'sons of Israel,' suggesting textual variants rather than theological translation; ἀγγέλων may reflect different Vorlage rather than interpretive choice.

revisions.mt_reading: Rejoice, O nations, his people (2 bicola in MT)
revisions.revised_reading: Rejoice, O heavens, with him, and let all the sons of God worship him. Rejoice, O nations, with his people, and let all the angels of God strengthen themselves in him (expanded 8 bicola)
revisions.evidence: LXX dramatically expands with heavenly liturgy, angelic worship, and cosmic rejoicing absent from MT. This expansion (confirmed by 4QDeut) likely reflects pre-LXX liturgical development emphasizing eschatological theophany and universal divine kingship. The LXX's retention of full expansion indicates deliberate theological choice to preserve cosmic-eschatological dimension.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek version greatly expands this verse to include heavenly beings and angels worshiping God, creating a grand cosmic scene of future divine victory that the shorter Hebrew text lacks.
scholarly_citations: Schaper, Eschatology in the Greek Psalter, p. 89
Skehan, The Structure of the Song of Moses, CBQ 1951
Wevers, Text History of the Greek Deuteronomy, p. 328
revisions.counter_arguments: The expansion likely existed in Hebrew Vorlage (4QDeut evidence), so LXX may simply preserve older text rather than create eschatological reading; MT represents truncation, not LXX expansion.

revisions.mt_reading: A wandering/perishing Aramean was my father (ʾărammî ʾōbēd ʾābî)
revisions.revised_reading: Laban the Aramean sought to destroy my father
revisions.evidence: Targum Onqelos and Neofiti reinterpret ʾōbēd (wandering/perishing) as active causative 'sought to destroy,' identifying 'Aramean' as Laban rather than Jacob. This reflects proto-rabbinic midrashic tradition (later codified in Passover Haggadah) reframing credo from Jacob's vulnerability to Laban's persecution. Represents halakhic concern to distinguish Israelite patriarch from Aramean identity.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew can mean 'my father was a wandering Aramean' (referring to Jacob). The Aramaic translation changes this to 'Laban the Aramean tried to destroy my father,' turning it into a story of persecution that later became central to Passover liturgy.
scholarly_citations: Kasher, Targum Onqelos on Deuteronomy 26, Tarbiz 1982
Shinan, The Aggadah of the Palestinian Targums, p. 156
Maher, Targum Pseudo-Jonathan: Deuteronomy, p. 87
revisions.counter_arguments: This could reflect legitimate lexical ambiguity in ʾōbēd and genuine uncertainty about referent rather than purely theological motivation; however, consistent Targumic pattern suggests interpretive agenda.

revisions.mt_reading: YHWH came from Sinai... from his right hand a fiery law (ʾēš dāt) for them
revisions.revised_reading: The LORD was revealed from Sinai... he gave to them the Law from between the angels
revisions.evidence: Targum Neofiti and Pseudo-Jonathan avoid MT's direct theophanic 'coming' with 'was revealed' (ʾitgəlî), distancing divine transcendence from spatial movement. More significantly, 'from his right hand' (divine body part) becomes 'from between the angels,' replacing anthropomorphic limb with angelic mediation of Torah. Reflects proto-rabbinic angelology of Sinai revelation.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew describes God literally coming from a place and giving law from his right hand. The Aramaic says God 'was revealed' (less physical) and gave the law through angels rather than from his own hand, avoiding human-like descriptions of God.
scholarly_citations: McNamara, Targum Neofiti 1: Deuteronomy, p. 143
Maher, Targum Pseudo-Jonathan: Deuteronomy, p. 119
revisions.counter_arguments: Angelic mediation at Sinai may reflect independent exegetical tradition (cf. Acts 7:53, Gal 3:19) rather than specifically anti-anthropomorphic motivation.

revisions.mt_reading: There was a king in Jeshurun (wayəhî bîšurûn melek)
revisions.revised_reading: And he shall be prince in the beloved one (ἐν τῷ ἠγαπημένῳ ἄρχων)
revisions.evidence: LXX's future tense ἔσται (he shall be) versus MT's past wayəhî introduces eschatological-messianic expectation. 'Prince' (ἄρχων) rather than βασιλεύς may reflect hesitancy about earthly kingship or messianic reserve. ἠγαπημένῳ (beloved) for Jeshurun may invoke Davidic covenant language (beloved = David, cf. 2 Sam 12:25). Pattern suggests messianic reading of Moses' blessing.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew says 'there was a king' (past tense) in Israel. The Greek changes this to future tense 'there shall be a prince,' possibly pointing forward to a coming messianic ruler and using language associated with King David.
scholarly_citations: Dogniez & Harl, La Bible d'Alexandrie: Le Deutéronome, p. 338
Barthélemy, Critique textuelle de l'Ancien Testament, vol. 1, p. 451
revisions.counter_arguments: Future tense may result from Greek grammatical convention or misreading of Hebrew consecutive perfect; ἄρχων is standard LXX rendering for melek without necessarily messianic connotation.

revisions.mt_reading: I kill and I make alive; I wound and I heal
revisions.revised_reading: I am He who kills the living in this world and makes alive the dead in the world to come; I smite those who sin and I heal them on the day of forgiveness
revisions.evidence: Targum Pseudo-Jonathan expands MT's terse affirmation of divine sovereignty over life/death into explicit resurrection theology ('world to come') and theodicy framework (sin-punishment-forgiveness). This reflects proto-rabbinic doctrine of ʿolam ha-baʾ and linkage of suffering to sin requiring atonement. Neofiti similarly adds 'world to come' for resurrection.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Hebrew simply says God kills and gives life. The Aramaic translation adds explicit references to resurrection of the dead in the future world and explains suffering as punishment for sin that God will heal through forgiveness.
scholarly_citations: Maher, Targum Pseudo-Jonathan: Deuteronomy, p. 114
Le Déaut, Targum du Pentateuque, vol. 5, p. 332
revisions.counter_arguments: Expansions in Pseudo-Jonathan are often late (post-Talmudic) and may not reflect formative proto-rabbinic period but medieval elaboration.

revisions.mt_reading: A prophet from among you, from your brothers... YHWH your God will raise up for you
revisions.revised_reading: A prophet from among your brothers... the Lord your God will raise up (ἀναστήσει) for you
revisions.evidence: While largely faithful rendering, LXX's ἀναστήσει carries stronger resurrection/eschatological connotation than MT qûm, especially given NT appropriation (Acts 3:22, 7:37). LXX's choice may reflect interpretive potential for singular eschatological prophet rather than succession of prophets. However, evidence is less strong than other cases as ἀνίστημι is standard for qûm.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Greek uses a verb for 'raise up' that can also mean 'resurrect,' which early Christians later interpreted as predicting Jesus. This may have been intentional, though the evidence is less clear than other cases.
scholarly_citations: Dogniez & Harl, La Bible d'Alexandrie: Le Deutéronome, p. 207
de Waard, A Greek Translation Technical Treatment, p. 89
revisions.counter_arguments: ἀνίστημι is entirely conventional LXX equivalent for hiphil of qûm without special eschatological force; messianic reading is retrospective NT interpretation, not LXX intention.

revisions.mt_reading: according to the number of the sons of God/Israel (textual variants)
revisions.revised_reading: according to the number of the sons of Israel
revisions.evidence: Samaritan Pentateuch reads bənê yiśrāʾēl, harmonizing with Israel's tribal divisions (cf. Gen 46) and avoiding both divine council theology (bənê ʾĕlōhîm in 4QDeut) and potential contradiction. SP characteristically harmonizes to remove theological ambiguity and narrative tension, here eliminating mythological overtones in favor of straightforward ethnic division matching Genesis genealogy.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Samaritan version changes 'sons of God' to 'sons of Israel,' making the verse simply say God divided nations according to Jacob's descendants, which matches the Genesis genealogy and avoids any hint of divine beings.
scholarly_citations: Tov, Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible, p. 83
Schorch, The Samaritan Version of Deuteronomy, p. 245
revisions.counter_arguments: SP reading may preserve original text against secondary expansion to bənê ʾĕlōhîm; harmonization charge assumes 4QDeut represents original rather than SP.

dominant_strategy: Anthropomorphism avoidance is most prominent, particularly in Targumic tradition which systematically employs Memra theology, divine 'revealing' rather than 'coming,' and angelic intermediaries to distance transcendent deity from spatial, corporeal, and emotional attributes. This reflects formative rabbinic theology's concern to preserve divine transcendence while maintaining covenant intimacy.
overall_assessment: Deuteronomy's textual traditions reveal diverse theological agendas: Targums prioritize anti-anthropomorphic revision and proto-rabbinic halakhic interpretation (resurrection theology, Laban midrash, angelology), reflecting Palestinian Jewish exegetical development. LXX exhibits selective eschatological sharpening (Deut 32:43 expansion, possible messianic reading in 33:5) alongside theological softening of potentially offensive material (4:19 astral cult, violent conquest language). SP demonstrates consistent harmonization removing internal Pentateuchal tensions. The pattern suggests Deuteronomy functioned as living liturgical-theological text across communities, each reshaping Moses' testament to address evolving theological concerns: Greek diaspora wrestling with Gentile cult contexts, Aramaic-speaking Jews developing rabbinic theology, and Samaritan tradents protecting textual-theological coherence of their restricted canon.
overall_plain: Different ancient communities translated Deuteronomy in ways that reflected their own theological concerns. Aramaic translators systematically removed human-like descriptions of God and added later rabbinic teachings about resurrection and the afterlife. Greek translators occasionally heightened messianic expectations and softened morally troubling passages. The Samaritan version smoothed out contradictions with other biblical books. These changes show Deuteronomy was not a static text but was continually reinterpreted to address each community's theological questions.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Targumic Memra Theology Reshapes Divine Agency in Deuteronomy
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The most significant finding is the systematic Targumic insertion of Memra theology throughout Deuteronomy's theophanic and divine action passages (9:3, 33:2, pervasively). This represents not isolated correction but comprehensive theological reframing, transforming direct divine-human encounter into mediated revelation. This strategy reveals formative rabbinic Judaism's solution to the tension between biblical anthropomorphism and emerging transcendence theology, creating interpretive framework that would dominate Jewish exegesis for centuries.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: Ancient Aramaic translators fundamentally changed how Deuteronomy describes God's relationship with Israel by inserting 'the Word of the Lord' as an intermediary, avoiding direct divine action. This became the standard Jewish way of reading the Bible for over a thousand years.
